steps.title: Use the horizontal-shift shortcut
steps.content: Data set Q is made by adding $540$ to every $x$-value in data set P. That shifts the scatterplot and its line of best fit $540$ units to the right. The slope stays the same; only the intercept changes.

steps.title: Shift a point on the original fit line
steps.content: From the original line of best fit for data set P, a readable point is about $(100,14)$. After the right shift, that point becomes $(640,14)$. The correct equation for Q should pass near $(640,14)$ and keep the original line's visible slope.

steps.title: Reject the bad matches
steps.content: Graph the wrong-looking choices against the shifted point. Choices B and C use the much flatter slope $\frac{1}{131}$, so they do not match the original best-fit line's slope. Choice D has the right steeper slope, but at $x=640$ it is far above the shifted point because $26+\frac{35}{1301}(640)\approx43.2$.

steps.title: Confirm the shifted model
steps.content: Choice A keeps the steeper slope and passes near the shifted point: $-3+\frac{35}{1301}(640)\approx14.2$. That matches data set Q, so choice A is correct.
